title: Idea genealogy & diffusion
subtitle: Search for an idea — and discover the path that connects it.

steps.title: An idea, at its origin
steps.body: 1982. A physicist describes how a network of simple units can store and recall memories as the stable states of an energy landscape — "Neural networks and physical systems with emergent collective computational abilities." We've parked on it, with its details open: the neighborhood and the links that fan out from it. This is where the idea begins.

steps.title: Cut to the backbone
steps.body: The raw neighborhood is noisy — decades of citations in every direction. Filter to the load-bearing papers: works only, high influence. What's left is the spine the idea travels along — and the springboard for finding where it went.

steps.title: Plot a course to a modern idea
steps.body: Does this 1982 model connect to today's AI? We can't see that from here — the answer lives in another field, decades on. So we search for it — "Attention Is All You Need" — and ask for a course from where we stand. A path exists, and it lights up: just two hops, through a single bridge paper. Nothing was teleported to; the citation graph drew this route.

steps.title: The bridge
steps.body: Before we travel, look at the paper in the middle — a 2020 review of where deep learning is headed. It's the one work that cites both Hopfield's 1982 model and the transformer, which is exactly why a path exists at all. This is the node that ties the two eras together.

steps.title: Where it ends up
steps.body: And the far end of the course: "Attention Is All You Need" — the architecture behind modern language models. A 1982 model of physical memory sits just two citations away from the transformer.

steps.title: Diffuse forward
steps.body: Now fly the path you discovered — out of neuroscience, through a 2020 engineering review, and into machine learning. The same idea, carried forward across forty years and three fields.

steps.title: Found, not given
steps.body: That's the difference: we didn't jump to an answer — we searched for an idea and discovered the path that ties it to where we started. One model of memory, traced from a 1982 physics paper to the transformer, by exploring the graph.
